variables.content:
At the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition point, where the zero-temperature effective mass diverges and the Landau effective-mass equation becomes homogeneous, the low-temperature solution for a homogeneous heavy-fermion liquid satisfies
      $
      M^{*}(T)\propto T^{-2/3}.
      $
      Therefore, in the non-Fermi-liquid regime near the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition, the quasiparticle effective mass decreases with increasing temperature according to the power law $T^{-2/3}$.

In the vicinity of the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition, well-defined quasiparticles continue to determine the low-temperature physics of strongly correlated Fermi systems, while the quasiparticle effective mass becomes a divergent function of temperature $T$, magnetic field $B$, and number density $x$.

The Landau paradigm based on (i) a quasiparticle description and (ii) an order parameter remains applicable to the low-energy physics of a heavy-fermion metallic electron liquid when the relevant zero-temperature control-parameter-driven quantum phase transition is the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT); in this setting the low-energy excitations are Landau quasiparticles with a renormalized effective mass M^{*}(x,T,B) that depends strongly on the carrier (electron) number density x, temperature T, and applied magnetic field B, and the ordered side of the transition is controlled by the momentum-dependent order parameter κ( p ) = sqrt{ n_0( p )(1-n_0( p )) }, where n_0( p ) is the T=0 quasiparticle momentum distribution in the condensate region.

The effective mass $M^{*}(T,B)$ is obtained by solving the coupled equations for the quasiparticle distribution and the Landau effective mass with a special form of the Landau interaction amplitude $F(\mathbf{p},\mathbf{p}_1)$ chosen so that the system is at the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition point. This choice requires the first two momentum derivatives of the single-particle spectrum $\varepsilon(\mathbf{p})$ to vanish at the Fermi momentum $p_F$.
[13]

Within the Landau-Fermi-liquid description of the spinon fluid there exists a fermion condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) quantum critical point at which the bare prefactor $1/M^{*}$ appearing in the Landau equation for the inverse quasiparticle effective mass tends to zero ($1/M^{*}\to 0$); at this point the linear (inhomogeneous) term in the integral equation for the inverse effective mass vanishes and the Landau equation becomes a homogeneous integral equation whose nontrivial solutions determine the temperature and field dependencies of the renormalized effective mass $M^{*}(T,B)$.
      [15]

A fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) in a fermionic electron system at zero temperature produces a fermion condensate (FC) that occupies a finite contiguous interval of single-particle momenta L_{\mathrm{FC}}=\{p: p_{i}\le p\le p_{f}\} in momentum space, where the superconducting order parameter \kappa(p) is nonzero and the single-particle occupation number n(p) satisfies 0<n(p)<1 for p\in L_{\mathrm{FC}} while n(p)=1 or n(p)=0 for momenta outside L_{\mathrm{FC}}; as a result the single-particle spectrum separates into two distinct quasiparticle subsystems: (i) quasiparticles with an anomalously large effective mass M_{\mathrm{FC}}^{*} that characterize excitations with momenta p\in L_{\mathrm{FC}}, and (ii) quasiparticles with a conventional finite effective mass M_{\mathrm{L}}^{*} that characterize excitations with p<p_{i}. If the superconducting pairing interaction kernel V(p_{1},p_{2}) has vanishing overall amplitude (V\to 0) then the single-particle dispersion within L_{\mathrm{FC}} becomes flat (zero slope) and the corresponding effective mass M_{\mathrm{FC}}^{*} diverges (M_{\mathrm{FC}}^{*}\to\infty), whereas a finite (but weak) pairing interaction makes M_{\mathrm{FC}}^{*} very large but finite.

Let $F^{1}(x)$ denote the $p$-wave component of the Landau interaction amplitude expressed as a function of particle density $x$, and let $N_0$ be the density of states entering the Landau effective-mass relation; the proposition is that if there exists a density $x_c$ such that
      $$
      1-\tfrac{1}{3}N_0 F^{1}(x_c)=0,
      $$
      then the denominator in the Landau effective-mass relation vanishes and the zero-temperature quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}(x)$ diverges at $x=x_c$, and that this divergence is identified with the fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT), provided no other competing instability (such as charge- or spin-density-wave formation, phase separation, or superconducting pairing) intervenes to cut off or preempt the divergence.

For a transition in La$_{2-x}$Sr$_x$CuO$_4$ that is controlled by disorder localization of bosonic carriers rather than by a Brinkman–Rice-type Mott–Hubbard mechanism acting on a fermionic Fermi surface, the interaction-driven enhancement of the single-particle effective mass expected near a Mott–Hubbard transition is absent.
      Imada 1993; Brinkman and Rice 1970

Let $m^*(U)$ denote the quasiparticle effective mass predicted by the Brinkman–Rice theory as a function of an interaction parameter $U$, which diverges at the theoretical Mott critical point in the homogeneous single-band model; the proposition is that the Brinkman–Rice scenario of a near-diverging effective mass $m^*$ as the Mott transition is approached applies qualitatively (and potentially quantitatively with caveats) to the experimentally prepared VO2 thin films that exhibit structural and electronic inhomogeneity, so that observed transport and optical signatures may be interpreted in terms of a Brinkman–Rice-like enhancement of $m^*$ in these inhomogeneous samples.

The fermion-condensation quantum phase transition (FCQPT) is defined as a zero-temperature many-body rearrangement of a fermionic system in which the Landau quasiparticle effective mass $M^*$ diverges so that $M/M^*\to 0$ (here $M$ denotes the bare electron mass and $M^*$ denotes the quasiparticle effective mass); at and near FCQPT the critical collective (order-parameter) fluctuations that typically characterize conventional quantum critical points are strongly suppressed so that well-defined fermionic quasiparticles persist as long-lived excitations down to arbitrarily low temperatures, and the dominant low-energy physics can therefore be described in terms of these quasiparticles with a strongly $T,B,x$ dependent effective mass $M^*(T,B,x)$.
      [17]
